statement: Spring Boot's core value proposition is convention over configuration: the framework makes opinionated default decisions so developers write zero XML and minimal properties files for standard use cases. If spring-data-jpa is on the classpath and a DataSource is configured, Spring Boot auto-configures an EntityManagerFactory, TransactionManager, and JPA repositories — all without a single @Bean declaration. The philosophy shift from Spring Framework is that opt-out (disable auto-config via exclude) is easier than opt-in (write configuration from scratch). This is why a production-ready REST service can start in 15 lines of code, and why the learning curve pitfall is not in starting but in understanding what the framework did implicitly when things go wrong.

Spring's Inversion of Control (IoC) container is its architectural foundation: instead of objects creating their own dependencies (new MyService()), the container creates and wires everything. Dependency Injection is the mechanism — objects declare what they need via constructor parameters, fields, or setters, and the container satisfies those needs. This inversion makes unit testing trivial (pass mocks in the constructor), decouples components (depend on interfaces, not implementations), and centralizes lifecycle management. Spring recommends constructor injection over field injection: constructor injection makes dependencies explicit, enables immutable fields (final), and surfaces circular dependencies at startup rather than at runtime.

Spring Boot auto-configuration works via the @EnableAutoConfiguration mechanism: at startup, Spring Boot reads META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports (Spring Boot 3.x) or META-INF/spring.factories (Spring Boot 2.x) to discover all @AutoConfiguration classes. Each class uses @ConditionalOnClass, @ConditionalOnMissingBean, and @ConditionalOnProperty annotations to apply configuration only when conditions are met. The order of auto-configurations matters and is controlled by @AutoConfigureBefore / @AutoConfigureAfter. To debug what was applied and why: run with --debug flag or set logging.level.org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure=DEBUG, which prints the AutoConfiguration report (CONDITIONS EVALUATION REPORT) listing every candidate and its match/no-match reason.

Circular dependency injection (A depends on B, B depends on A) causes BeanCurrentlyInCreationException at startup with constructor injection — Spring cannot satisfy both at the same time. With field injection (@Autowired), Spring historically resolved this silently using three-level cache (early-reference singletons), masking design problems. Spring Boot 2.6+ made circular dependencies a startup failure by default (spring.main.allow-circular-references defaults to false) precisely to force architectural fixes. The correct fix is redesign: extract a third component C that both A and B depend on, or use @Lazy on one injection point as a last resort (defers proxy resolution but does not fix the underlying coupling). Seeing this error is a signal that the service boundary is wrong.

@Transactional works through Spring AOP proxies: when you inject a @Service bean, you get a proxy, and calling a @Transactional method through the proxy triggers transaction management. But if a method inside the same class calls another @Transactional method on `this`, the call bypasses the proxy entirely — the inner method runs without a transaction (or inherits whatever the outer context is). This is the most notorious Spring bug: developers add @Transactional to an internal method, see no error, but no transaction is created. Fixes: (a) move the inner method to a separate @Service bean and inject it; (b) inject the bean into itself via @Autowired (self-injection) — ugly but works; (c) use AspectJ weaving instead of proxy-based AOP. The root cause is proxy-based AOP's fundamental limitation: it only intercepts calls that cross the proxy boundary.

Spring Security is implemented as a chain of servlet filters registered with a specific order. The SecurityFilterChain defines which URLs are intercepted and which filters apply. A critical ordering gotcha: if you define multiple SecurityFilterChain beans (common for separating API auth from web form auth), they must have explicit @Order annotations — lower number = higher priority. Without explicit ordering, both chains match all requests and behavior is undefined. Common pattern: @Order(1) for JWT/API chain that matches /api/**, @Order(2) for form-login chain that matches everything else. Also important: filters run before @ControllerAdvice exception handling, so security exceptions (401/403) must be handled via AuthenticationEntryPoint and AccessDeniedHandler, not @ExceptionHandler.

The JPA N+1 query problem: if you fetch a list of 100 Order entities and each Order has a lazy-loaded List<OrderItem>, accessing orderItem fields in a loop triggers 100 additional SELECT queries (1 for orders + N for items). This is silent — no exception, just catastrophic performance at scale. Solutions in priority order: (1) JPQL JOIN FETCH: `SELECT o FROM Order o JOIN FETCH o.items` — fetches everything in one query; (2) @EntityGraph on the repository method — declarative join fetch without modifying JPQL; (3) @BatchSize(size=N) — fetches N collections per batch, reducing N+1 to ceil(N/batch)+1 queries; (4) projections/DTOs via constructor expressions — bypass entity graph entirely when you only need a subset of fields. The general rule: never leave bidirectional associations as FetchType.LAZY without a strategy for the collection access pattern.

Spring Boot Actuator exposes production-readiness endpoints over HTTP or JMX. Key endpoints: /actuator/health (liveness/readiness — critical for Kubernetes probes), /actuator/metrics (Micrometer integration for Prometheus), /actuator/info (build/git metadata), /actuator/env (resolved configuration values), /actuator/loggers (change log level at runtime without restart), /actuator/threaddump and /actuator/heapdump for debugging live issues. Security note: Actuator endpoints are sensitive — expose only /health and /info publicly; protect the rest behind management.server.port on a non-public network interface. The management.endpoints.web.exposure.include property defaults to only 'health' in production; explicit opt-in prevents accidentally exposing internal state. Actuator's Micrometer integration is the preferred path to metrics rather than custom instrumentation.

Spring Boot's profile-based configuration allows environment-specific properties via application-{profile}.properties (or .yml). Active profiles are set via SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE env var, --spring.profiles.active command-line arg, or spring.profiles.active in application.properties. Key design principles: (1) application.properties holds universal defaults; profile files override only what differs; (2) multiple profiles can be active simultaneously (comma-separated), with later profiles winning on conflict; (3) @Profile on @Configuration/@Bean controls which beans exist in each environment; (4) Spring Boot 2.4+ introduced config trees and importable config files — avoid mixing old-style spring.profiles with new-style spring.config.activate.on-profile. Credentials should never be in profile files; use environment variables or Spring Cloud Config / Vault integration.

Spring MVC (servlet stack) uses one thread per request from a thread pool; threads block during I/O (DB calls, HTTP calls). Spring WebFlux (reactive stack) uses an event loop with non-blocking I/O; a small number of threads handle thousands of concurrent requests without blocking. The correct choice: use WebFlux when your application is genuinely I/O-bound and you need high concurrency with minimal threads (API gateway, streaming, chat), or when integrating with reactive data stores (R2DBC, Reactive MongoDB). Use MVC when: your team is unfamiliar with reactive programming (debugging a Flux stack trace is significantly harder), your data store is JPA/JDBC (no reactive driver), or you use Spring Security features not yet ported to WebFlux. Mixing the stacks is possible but discouraged. Reactive does not automatically mean faster — on low-concurrency CRUD apps it adds overhead for zero gain.

Spring Bean lifecycle order: constructor → @Autowired field/setter injection → @PostConstruct → ApplicationListener<ContextRefreshedEvent>. Common ordering pitfall: @PostConstruct runs before the full application context is ready, so beans that use ApplicationEventPublisher or interact with other beans that have their own @PostConstruct may encounter uninitialized state. Prefer implementing SmartInitializingSingleton (afterSingletonsInstantiated()) when you need all singletons to be initialized first. For startup tasks, ApplicationRunner and CommandLineRunner run after the full context is refreshed, making them the safest hooks for data seeding, cache warming, or external service handshakes. @PreDestroy runs during graceful shutdown — use for connection cleanup, but set spring.lifecycle.timeout-per-shutdown-phase to give it enough time.

Spring Boot starters (spring-boot-starter-web, spring-boot-starter-data-jpa, etc.) are dependency aggregators — they declare transitive dependencies at versions known to work together, managed by the Spring Boot BOM (Bill of Materials) via spring-boot-dependencies. This means you rarely specify versions for Spring ecosystem libraries. The trade-off: (1) version conflicts arise when you mix Spring Boot-managed libraries with third-party libraries that have their own version constraints — use `mvn dependency:tree` or `gradle dependencies` to trace conflicts; (2) upgrading Spring Boot major version (2.x→3.x) can cascade breaking changes: Jakarta EE namespace (javax→jakarta), removal of deprecated APIs, Hibernate 6 dialect changes. The discipline is: let the BOM manage versions, override only when you have a documented reason, and always run integration tests after BOM upgrades.

Spring WebFlux reactive pipelines silently block the event loop when code calls a blocking method—JDBC, Files.readAllBytes, Thread.sleep—inside a Mono/Flux operator. The event loop has a fixed small thread pool, so one blocking call stalls all concurrent requests on that thread. The failure is invisible under low load and catastrophic at moderate concurrency. The correct pattern is to wrap all blocking calls in Schedulers.boundedElastic() via publishOn or subscribeOn. Tools like BlockHound can detect blocking calls in reactive context at test time and should be enabled in CI.

Spring Security's CSRF protection is enabled by default for stateful (session-based) apps but disabled by default for stateless (JWT/API) apps when using the lambda DSL. The hidden failure is that teams extending the wrong SecurityFilterChain base class or missing the csrf.disable() call either block legitimate API clients with 403 CSRF errors or unintentionally expose session-based endpoints to CSRF attacks. The discipline is to always explicitly declare CSRF policy—either csrf.disable() for pure APIs or csrfTokenRepository for session apps—rather than relying on defaults that change between Spring Security major versions.

JPA's @OneToOne with fetch = LAZY is silently treated as EAGER when the association is on the owning side without a @LazyToOne hint. Hibernate cannot make a lazy proxy for the owning side of a @OneToOne because it must check whether the associated row exists. The consequence is N+1 loads for a relationship developers believe is lazy. The fix is to use Bytecode Enhancement or restructure to @OneToMany with a unique constraint when lazy loading on a @OneToOne owning side is performance-critical.

@SpringBootTest starts the full application context including all beans, auto-configuration, and embedded server, making it the most realistic but slowest test type. The correct layered testing strategy is: @WebMvcTest for controller layer only (security, validation, serialization), @DataJpaTest for repository layer only (actual SQL via H2 or Testcontainers), and @SpringBootTest only for integration tests verifying cross-layer behavior. Each slice annotation loads a restricted context subset. Overusing @SpringBootTest for what should be unit tests creates test suites that take minutes and fail for unrelated bean initialization reasons.

GraalVM Native Image compilation for Spring Boot 3 requires all reflective access, proxy generation, and resource loading to be declared up front via @RegisterReflectionForBinding, @ImportRuntimeHints, or native-image.properties. The hidden payoff is startup times under 100ms and minimal memory footprint—critical for serverless and sidecar deployments. The hidden cost is that third-party libraries that use reflection without providing native hints break at runtime, not compile time. The discipline is to run the full test suite against the native binary in CI and to check Spring's third-party library compatibility matrix before upgrading.

Testcontainers with Spring Boot allows integration tests to spin up real databases, Kafka brokers, or Redis instances in Docker. The hidden failure is that each test class annotated with @SpringBootTest creates a new ApplicationContext and potentially a new container unless @DynamicPropertySource and container reuse are configured correctly. Without reuse, a test suite with 20 integration test classes starts 20 Postgres containers sequentially, adding minutes to CI. The correct pattern is to use a shared static container with Testcontainers' reuse flag or Spring Boot 3.1's ServiceConnection auto-configuration which handles container lifecycle sharing automatically.

Spring's @Async annotation creates a fire-and-forget proxy call but silently swallows exceptions unless a custom AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler is configured. The default behavior logs the exception at ERROR level and discards it. Business operations marked @Async—sending emails, audit logging, notifications—fail without any caller visibility. The correct pattern is to always configure an AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler that persists failures to a retry queue or alerts, and to return CompletableFuture<Void> from @Async methods in contexts where the caller needs to know about failure.

Spring Boot's default connection pool (HikariCP) has a default pool size of 10 connections. Under moderate load, a service making synchronous database calls from a thread-per-request model saturates the pool and queues threads waiting for connections. The hidden constraint is that increasing pool size is not always the correct fix: oversized pools can overwhelm the database. The correct analysis is to measure connection wait time in Actuator's hikaricp.connections.pending metric, ensure database-side max_connections is at least sum of all service pool sizes, and consider read replicas or caching before simply raising pool limits.

Spring Batch is the correct framework for large-scale ETL in Spring Boot applications because it provides chunk-oriented processing, restartability from the last successful checkpoint, and job execution metadata out of the box. The hidden pattern is using StepExecutionListener and ItemProcessor pipelines to keep transformation logic testable in isolation. Teams that implement large data migrations as @Scheduled methods or @Async tasks discover after the first failure that they have no restart capability, no partial progress tracking, and no retry semantics—exactly what Spring Batch provides by default.

Micrometer metrics registered with @Timed or Timer.record() require the application to have at least one request or method invocation before the metric appears in Prometheus or Datadog. Newly deployed services that have not yet received traffic show empty dashboards, not zero-value metrics. Alerting rules that fire on missing metrics will page immediately after a clean deploy. The correct pattern is to eagerly register all meters at startup with a zero value using MeterRegistry.gauge() or counter() initialization, and to document this behavior in runbook so on-call engineers understand that missing metrics at startup are expected.
